---
title: "Properties of MLE: Uncertainty in Parameters"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 3 · Lecture B — Properties of the MLE"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---


## Learning outcomes {.smaller}

By the end of this module you should be able to:

1. **Show the MLE solution is unique** — that the log-likelihood has a single
   maximum (a negative-definite Hessian).
2. **State what unbiasedness of $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ does and does not mean** — and
   the common claims it is *not*.
3. **Quantify parameter uncertainty** — via $\mathrm{Cov}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}]$ and
   the Fisher information.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: three properties, one per outcome — uniqueness, unbiasedness,
uncertainty. The checkpoint gates on reproducing the first two arguments.
:::

## Recap: likelihood and the MLE {.smaller}

The linear-Gaussian likelihood:

$$
p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)
\;=\; \prod_{n=1}^{N} \mathcal{N}\!\left(y_n \,\middle|\, \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n,\ \sigma^2\right)
$$

Its maximizers — the MLE (derived in the likelihood lecture of this module):

$$
\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y},
\qquad
\hat{\sigma}^2 = \tfrac{1}{N}\left(\mathbf{y}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\hat{\mathbf{w}}\right)
$$

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: established in this module's likelihood lecture; today the question
is — how good is $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$? Is it unique, is it unbiased, and how
uncertain is it?
:::

# Unique, unbiased, and how uncertain. {.center}

## Property 1 · Uniqueness {.smaller}

The log-likelihood — we write it $\ell(\mathbf{w}) = \log p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)$,
reserving $\mathcal{L}$ for losses:

$$
\ell(\mathbf{w}) = -\tfrac{N}{2}\log 2\pi \;-\; N\log\sigma
\;-\; \tfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\left(y_n - \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n\right)^2
$$

::: {.fragment}
Gradient — vanishes at the normal equations:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mathbf{w}}
= \tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\right)
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
Hessian — constant and **negative definite**
($\mathbf{v}^{\!\top}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{v} < 0\ \ \forall\,\mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}$):

$$
\mathbf{H} \;=\; \frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial \mathbf{w}\,\partial \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}}
\;=\; -\tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\,\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{a single, unique maximum.}
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
[The same $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ full-column-rank condition from Module 1 —
now reappearing as a negative-definite Hessian.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: negative-definite everywhere ⇒ strictly concave ⇒ at most one
stationary point, and it is the global max. Ties uniqueness to rank of X.
:::

::: {.notes}

redrawn clean in theme colors. The punchline is the last fragment: uncertainty in

## Expectation — a reminder {.smaller}

<!-- CUTTABLE: prereq recap (async L09a). First slide to drop if the deck runs
     long; nothing downstream depends on it. -->

::: {style="border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:10px; padding:0.4em 1em; background:var(--surface);"}
For a random vector $\mathbf{x}$ with density $p(\mathbf{x})$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{x})}[f(\mathbf{x})] = \int f(\mathbf{x})\,p(\mathbf{x})\,d\mathbf{x}
$$

**Mean** $\;\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}]\qquad$
**Covariance** $\;\mathrm{Cov}[\mathbf{x}] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{\!\top}\right]$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: prereq recap (async L09a) — keep brief. We only need linearity of
expectation and the covariance definition for the next two slides.
:::

## Property 2 · Unbiasedness {.smaller}

Take the expectation of $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ over datasets from the generating
process (only $\mathbf{y}$ is random; $\mathbf{X}$ is fixed):

::: {.fragment}
$$
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}] = \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\,\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}]
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
$$
= \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
$$
= \mathbf{w} \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad \hat{\mathbf{w}}\ \text{is unbiased.}
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: uses $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}] = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}$ (the model mean).
The estimator is centered on the true $\mathbf{w}$ — on average across datasets.
:::

## What "unbiased" means — and doesn't {.smaller}

**Unbiased means:** across repeated size-$N$ samples from the generating
process, the collection of estimates $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ is **centered** on the
true $\mathbf{w}$.

It is **not** the same as:

- **"more data ⇒ better estimates"** — true here, but that is a *separate*
  sample-size effect, not what unbiasedness asserts;
- **UMVU** (uniformly minimum-variance unbiased) — a strictly stronger claim
  about being *closest*, which unbiasedness alone does not give;
- a guarantee of being best for *your* dataset — a **biased** estimator can land
  closer to $\mathbf{w}$ from *less* data.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: this is the slide students misremember — keep the three "not"s
crisp. Unbiasedness is a statement about the average, not any single fit.
:::

## Property 3 · Covariance

The punchline first — the parameter covariance is

$$
\boxed{\;\mathrm{Cov}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}] \;=\; \sigma^2\left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\;}
$$

Derived in full in the appendix → [appendix-covariance.html](appendix-covariance.html).

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: small $\sigma^2$ (low noise) or large/well-conditioned
$\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ (more, more-spread data) ⇒ tighter estimates. The
full $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}\hat{\mathbf{w}}^{\!\top}]$ chain is the appendix.
:::

## Fisher information {.smaller}

The (expected) Fisher information is the curvature of the log-likelihood:

$$
\mathcal{I} \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[-\,\frac{\partial^2 \log p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)}{\partial \mathbf{w}\,\partial \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}}\right]
\;=\; \tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\,\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\mathrm{Cov}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}] \;=\; \mathcal{I}^{-1}
$$

- **Diagonal** $\mathcal{I}_{jj}$ — information about each parameter on its own;
- **Off-diagonal** $\mathcal{I}_{jk}$ — information *shared* between a pair (their
  estimates trade off).

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: more information ⇒ smaller inverse ⇒ tighter covariance. Note the
Hessian from Property 1 reappears here — same $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$.
:::

## Parameter uncertainty on NHANES

::: {#cell-fig-cov-ellipses .cell execution_count=2}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Cov[ŵ] = σ̂²(XᵀX)⁻¹ shrinks as N grows: N = 40 (orange, dashed) vs. full N (green, solid).](m3b-mle-uncertainty_files/figure-revealjs/fig-cov-ellipses-output-1.svg){#fig-cov-ellipses fig-alt='Two panels. Left: scatter of systolic BP against standardized age for NHANES adults in blue with a vermillion least-squares fit line. Right: the parameter plane of intercept w0 versus slope w1, showing two 2-sigma covariance ellipses — a wide dashed orange ellipse for a small sample of N=40 and a much smaller solid green ellipse for the full sample, both centered on the full-data estimate, illustrating that more data gives a tighter parameter covariance.'}
:::
:::


## Live demo: the parameter-uncertainty explorer {background-iframe="https://gchism94.github.io/info521-activities-2026/m3-parameter-uncertainty/" background-interactive="true"}

Draw datasets from a fixed process — watch $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ scatter and fill the
$\mathrm{Cov}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}]$ ellipse, live on this slide.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: full-bleed interactive embed of the M3 parameter-uncertainty demo
(PRIMARY pattern, CLAUDE.md). In-lecture demo only — M3 has no peer loop. The
covariance-ellipse figure on the previous slide is the print / PDF fallback.
:::

## Through-line {.smaller}

::: {style="color: var(--accent); font-weight:650;"}
Uniqueness (negative-definite Hessian) → Fisher information $\mathcal{I} = \tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ → (Module 5) the Laplace approximation's posterior covariance $\mathbf{H}^{-1} = \mathcal{I}^{-1}$.
:::

The curvature of the log-likelihood is one object doing three jobs, three modules
apart — **same $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$, same inverse.**

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: plant this now; in Module 5 the Laplace posterior covariance is
literally this $\mathcal{I}^{-1}$. Students should feel the callback land.
:::

## Recap + checkpoint preview {.smaller}

Three properties of the linear-Gaussian MLE:

1. **Unique** — $\mathbf{H} = -\tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ is negative definite.
2. **Unbiased** — $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}] = \mathbf{w}$.
3. **Uncertain, quantifiably** — $\mathrm{Cov}[\hat{\mathbf{w}}] = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \mathcal{I}^{-1}$.

The **P1-M2 closed-book checkpoint** gates on reproducing the **uniqueness** and
**unbiasedness** arguments from scratch.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: bridge to the checkpoint. Students should be able to reproduce the
Hessian sign argument and the three-line unbiasedness derivation unaided.
:::
